title: Chip-carved oak panel
subtitle: A repeating geometric pattern in chip-carved oak for a small decorative panel or furniture insert
excerpt: Chip carving cuts a geometric pattern into a flat board surface by removing small triangular chips with two angled knife cuts. Oak takes a chip cut cleanly and the cut surfaces appear polished against the uncut background. A repeating six-point rosette or grid pattern is the standard beginner chip design: each unit is composed of three cuts, all at the same angle, making it easy to maintain consistency across a full panel. This intermediate project covers setting out the grid, the three-cut chip, and finishing the oak surface.
sourceNotes: The Art Amateur vol. 47 (1902) for chip carving patterns and technique. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the geometric tradition in English decorative carving.

glossaryTerms.definition: A V-shaped cut made by two angled knife strokes that meet at the bottom of the V to release a triangular chip. The two cuts are made at the same angle to the surface; the depth at the apex determines the relief.
A straight downward cut across the grain that defines the boundary of a chip. The stop cut is made first; the angled undercut releases the chip to the stop-cut wall.
Fully dried timber. Chip carving requires a seasoned board; green or damp timber will not release a chip cleanly and will shrink after carving, opening the cut marks unevenly.

content.text: Chip carving is the oldest continuous decorative woodwork tradition in Britain: the same
chip-cut
rosette and interlace patterns appear in medieval church furniture, seventeenth-century oak chests, and Victorian cottage decoration. On
seasoned

oak, the cut surfaces have a polished, faceted quality that contrasts sharply with the uncut background; lime and cherry produce a similar effect with less effort because they are softer. The key discipline is keeping the knife angle consistent: every chip cut in the pattern must be made at the same angle to the surface or the relief will look uneven.
What you need: wood

A flat, seasoned oak panel, 20 cm by 15 cm, 12 mm thick. The surface must be planed flat; a rough-sawn surface will not give a clean chip cut because the knife angle cannot be held consistently over an uneven background. Lime and cherry are easier starting species; lime in particular is the traditional European chip-carving wood.
What you need: tools

A chip-carving knife with a short, stiff blade. A try square and marking gauge for setting out the grid. A leather strop to maintain the edge throughout; a chip carving knife dulls quickly on oak and must be stropped after every 10 to 15 chips.
Safety
Hold the panel on a non-slip mat or grip it with a carving clamp. The chip-carving knife is pushed away from the body on the angled stroke; keep the free hand clear of the cut line.
The work
Step 1: Set out the grid

With the try square and marking gauge, draw a grid of squares at 20 mm intervals across the full panel surface. Mark the diagonal lines of each square; the intersection of these diagonals gives the centre of each chip unit. A six-point rosette uses six triangular chips arranged around the centre point; a simple repeating pattern uses one chip per square.
Step 2: Cut the stop cuts
For each triangular chip, make the
stop cut
first: press the knife straight down along the deepest point of the triangle (the apex), perpendicular to the surface. This cut defines the wall from which the chip will be released.
Step 3: Make the chip cuts

Hold the chip-carving knife at 45 degrees to the surface. Push the blade into the wood along the first side of the triangle, angling downward toward the stop-cut apex. The chip should lift cleanly as the blade reaches the stop cut. Repeat on the second side. Both cuts meet at the stop cut; if the chip does not release cleanly, the stop cut was not deep enough. Re-cut the stop cut slightly deeper and repeat.
Step 4: Work across the panel

Complete all stop cuts across the full panel before making any chip cuts; this allows the stop cuts to be made consistently while the grain is still unbroken. Then complete the chip cuts in sequence. Strop the knife before each row of chips.
Finishing

Sand the flat background areas only with 180 and 240 grit, keeping the sandpaper flat and away from the chip edges. Do not sand inside the cut surfaces; the faceted, slightly polished cut faces are the visual contrast the pattern relies on. Leave the oak unfinished or apply a very thin wipe of raw linseed oil; heavy oil application darkens the cut faces and reduces the contrast.
Variations
A six-point rosette uses six chips arranged around a centre with one stop cut per chip meeting at the centre point. The effect is a star or flower in low relief.
Lime (Tilia) carves with less effort than oak and is the traditional choice for fine decorative panels; the pale, even-grained surface shows the cut faces very clearly.

items.symptom: The chip tears out rather than lifting cleanly
items.cause: The knife is dull, or the cut was made against the grain.
items.fix: Strop the knife and check the grain direction on the panel; chip cuts that go across or against the grain tear rather than slice. Rotate the panel so the cut is always made with the grain where possible.

items.symptom: The chip depths are uneven across the panel
items.cause: The knife angle changed between chips.
items.fix: Mark a 45-degree guide line on the knife handle with a felt pen and check it against the surface at the start of each row.
